result:
The kettle clicks off and the kitchen fills with that silver-dart pause just before the first bird gives up the dawn. Steam coils from the spout like a gentle dragon who has forgotten what to burn, and the mug I choose—chipped rim, glaze crazed into tiny rivers—snugs exactly four fingers inside its handle, as though it has been waiting for this particular dawn and these fingers for years. Outside, the hedge holds a single pearl of dew at the tip of every leaf. If I stare long enough the pearls detach themselves from vision and become a slow-moving constellation, each droplet the world’s tiniest planet catching a piece of sunrise fire.  

Inside my skull drafts are the same: half-formed islands floating to the surface, some already with campsites of verbs staking out territory, others nothing but blank watercolor smudges. I stir too much honey into the cup; it settles like sunlight on pulp. Taste brings back a memory that has no face or year, only weather—late-August heat and the squeak of bike brakes on gravel. Somewhere between that taste and this steaming present I misplace time entirely, and suddenly it is not 7:03 a.m. or 2024 but one long golden seam stitching everything I’ve ever noticed. The cat noses the back door. Birds erase themselves and start again. I sip, holding the ordinary heat on my tongue long enough to memorize the weight of gravity and the short, exquisite distance between hand and lip.

message.content:
The kettle clicks off and the kitchen fills with that silver-dart pause just before the first bird gives up the dawn. Steam coils from the spout like a gentle dragon who has forgotten what to burn, and the mug I choose—chipped rim, glaze crazed into tiny rivers—snugs exactly four fingers inside its handle, as though it has been waiting for this particular dawn and these fingers for years. Outside, the hedge holds a single pearl of dew at the tip of every leaf. If I stare long enough the pearls detach themselves from vision and become a slow-moving constellation, each droplet the world’s tiniest planet catching a piece of sunrise fire.  

Inside my skull drafts are the same: half-formed islands floating to the surface, some already with campsites of verbs staking out territory, others nothing but blank watercolor smudges. I stir too much honey into the cup; it settles like sunlight on pulp. Taste brings back a memory that has no face or year, only weather—late-August heat and the squeak of bike brakes on gravel. Somewhere between that taste and this steaming present I misplace time entirely, and suddenly it is not 7:03 a.m. or 2024 but one long golden seam stitching everything I’ve ever noticed. The cat noses the back door. Birds erase themselves and start again. I sip, holding the ordinary heat on my tongue long enough to memorize the weight of gravity and the short, exquisite distance between hand and lip.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
